systems.name: Atomic Design
systems.description: Brad Frost's methodology that breaks UI into five hierarchical levels: atoms, molecules, organisms, templates, and pages. Provides a clear mental model for building component libraries from the smallest indivisible elements up to full page compositions.
principles: Build from the smallest indivisible UI elements upward
Each level composes elements from the level below
Templates define layout structure without real content
Pages are specific instances of templates with real content
Components should be context-agnostic at lower levels
when_to_use: Building a component library or design system from scratch
Team needs shared vocabulary for component granularity
Large-scale application with many reusable UI pieces
Establishing a pattern library for multi-product organization
avoid_when: Small project with few reusable components
Team is unfamiliar with component-based architecture
Rapid prototype where formality slows iteration
Single-page marketing site with no recurring patterns
signals: Codebase has components at mixed granularity with no naming convention
Designers and developers disagree on what constitutes a component
Pattern library lacks organizational hierarchy
Component reuse is low despite visual repetition
key_concepts: Atoms: buttons, labels, inputs, icons
Molecules: search bar (input + button), form field (label + input + error)
Organisms: navigation header, product card grid, footer
Templates: page-level layout scaffolding without real data
Pages: template instances with actual content

systems.name: Design Thinking
systems.description: IDEO's human-centered design methodology that emphasizes empathy with users, clear problem definition, broad ideation, rapid prototyping, and iterative testing. Prioritizes understanding real human needs before jumping to solutions.
principles: Empathy is the foundation of all design decisions
Define the right problem before solving
Diverge broadly before converging on solutions
Bias toward action through rapid prototyping
Fail early, fail fast, learn quickly
when_to_use: Complex problem space with unclear user needs
New product or feature with high uncertainty
Innovation projects requiring fresh perspectives
Cross-functional team alignment on user problems
avoid_when: Requirements are well-defined and validated
Tight deadline with no room for user research
Incremental UI polish on existing patterns
Technical infrastructure work with no user-facing impact
signals: Stakeholders disagree on what users actually need
Features are being built without user validation
Product-market fit is uncertain
Team is solution-jumping before understanding the problem
key_concepts: Empathize: interviews, observation, journey mapping
Define: problem statements, How Might We questions
Ideate: brainstorming, crazy eights, dot voting
Prototype: low-fidelity mockups, paper prototypes, clickable wireframes
Test: usability testing, A/B testing, user feedback sessions

systems.name: Double Diamond
systems.description: British Design Council's framework with two phases of divergent and convergent thinking. The first diamond explores the problem space (Discover and Define). The second diamond explores the solution space (Develop and Deliver). Ensures teams solve the right problem before solving it right.
principles: Diverge before converging in each phase
First diamond: find the right problem
Second diamond: find the right solution
Be people-centred throughout
Communicate visually and iterate relentlessly
when_to_use: Service design or organizational design projects
Complex systems with multiple stakeholder groups
Policy design requiring research and synthesis
Projects where the problem itself is unclear
avoid_when: Single-component visual redesign
Well-scoped feature request with clear acceptance criteria
Pure visual polish or brand refresh
Time-boxed sprint work with defined tickets
signals: Team is oscillating between problem and solution without structure
Stakeholders want to jump to solutions immediately
Research insights are not being synthesized into actionable problem statements
Design process lacks clear phases or milestones
key_concepts: Discover: broad research, ethnography, market analysis
Define: synthesis, insight clustering, problem framing
Develop: co-design, prototyping, concept testing
Deliver: final testing, implementation, launch, measurement

systems.name: User-Centered Design (UCD)
systems.description: ISO 9241-210 iterative process that places users at the center of every design decision. Involves understanding usage context, specifying user requirements, producing design solutions, and evaluating against requirements in repeating cycles.
principles: Design is based upon explicit understanding of users, tasks, and environments
Users are involved throughout design and development
Design is driven and refined by user-centered evaluation
Process is iterative
Design addresses the whole user experience
when_to_use: Product development requiring regulatory compliance
Enterprise software with complex user workflows
Medical, aviation, or safety-critical interfaces
Redesigning existing product based on usability findings
avoid_when: Building developer tools where designers are the users
Internal tooling with direct access to users for informal feedback
Rapid experimental prototyping with no defined user base
Infrastructure or backend work with no UI
signals: Usability issues are discovered late in development
User research is disconnected from design decisions
Product requirements are based on stakeholder opinions not user data
No iterative evaluation cycle exists
key_concepts: Context of use analysis
User requirements specification
Design solution production
Evaluation against requirements
Iterative refinement cycles

systems.name: Lean UX
systems.description: Jeff Gothelf's approach emphasizing outcomes over deliverables. Replaces heavy documentation with hypotheses, MVPs, and validated learning. Tightly integrates with agile development to get designs in front of users as fast as possible.
principles: Outcomes over outputs
Hypotheses over requirements
Experiments over specifications
Cross-functional collaboration over handoffs
Validated learning over opinion
when_to_use: Agile teams needing lightweight design process
Startups validating product-market fit
Feature experiments requiring rapid iteration
Teams where designers and developers work in same sprint
avoid_when: Regulated industries requiring documentation
Complex enterprise systems needing thorough specification
Mission-critical interfaces where failure is costly
Large-scale design system creation requiring comprehensive specs
signals: Design documentation is never read by developers
Weeks pass between design and user validation
Team treats mockups as final specifications
No measurement framework for design success
key_concepts: Assumption mapping and prioritization
Hypothesis statements: We believe [outcome] for [persona] if [feature]
Minimum viable product (MVP) for testing
Build-Measure-Learn feedback loop
Collaborative design studios

systems.name: Jobs to Be Done (JTBD)
systems.description: Framework for understanding customer motivations through the lens of functional, emotional, and social jobs they hire products to do. Shifts focus from demographics to progress-making forces: push, pull, anxiety, and habit.
principles: People hire products to make progress in their lives
Jobs are stable over time even as solutions change
Every job has functional, emotional, and social dimensions
Competition is defined by the job, not the product category
Understanding the struggling moment reveals innovation opportunities
when_to_use: Defining product strategy and positioning
Identifying unmet user needs for new features
Understanding why users switch between products
Prioritizing feature development based on user progress
avoid_when: Purely visual design decisions
Micro-interaction design within established patterns
Technical architecture decisions
Component-level design system work
signals: Feature requests that don't align with clear user outcomes
Product roadmap driven by competitor features rather than user jobs
High churn with unclear reasons
Users adopting workarounds for tasks the product should handle
key_concepts: Functional job: the practical task to accomplish
Emotional job: how the user wants to feel
Social job: how the user wants to be perceived
Push forces: dissatisfaction with current solution
Pull forces: attraction to new solution
Anxieties: fears about switching
Habits: attachment to current behavior

systems.name: Gestalt Principles of Visual Perception
systems.description: Psychological principles describing how humans perceive visual elements as organized patterns and groups. Foundational to layout, grouping, hierarchy, and visual communication in interface design.
principles: The whole is perceived as more than the sum of its parts
Humans naturally seek patterns and order
Visual grouping reduces cognitive load
Perception is influenced by context and surroundings
Simplicity and regularity are preferred interpretations
when_to_use: Designing layouts and information hierarchy
Grouping related UI elements without explicit borders
Creating visual flow and reading order
Evaluating why a layout feels disorganized or confusing
avoid_when: Principle application would conflict with accessibility needs
Over-reliance on implicit grouping when explicit labels are needed
Cultural context where perception patterns differ significantly
signals: Users cannot identify related elements on a page
Layout feels cluttered despite adequate whitespace
Form fields and labels are visually disconnected
Navigation structure does not match visual grouping
key_concepts: Proximity: elements close together are perceived as a group
Similarity: elements sharing visual traits are perceived as related
Closure: the mind completes incomplete shapes
Continuity: the eye follows smooth paths and lines
Figure-ground: elements are perceived as either foreground or background
Common region: elements within a boundary are perceived as grouped
Common fate: elements moving together are perceived as a unit

systems.name: Material Design 3 (Material You)
systems.description: Google's comprehensive design system emphasizing personal expression through dynamic color, updated components, and adaptive layouts. M3 introduces dynamic color extraction from wallpapers, a refined token system, and full support for large screens and foldables.
principles: Personal: dynamic color adapts to user preference
Adaptive: responsive across all screen sizes and form factors
Expressive: updated shape, color, and typography for emotional range
Accessible: built-in accessibility with WCAG compliance
Unified: consistent cross-platform experience
when_to_use: Android or cross-platform applications
Products requiring strong theming and personalization
Applications targeting material ecosystem consistency
Projects needing comprehensive component library with tokens
avoid_when: Apple-only applications where HIG is expected
Brand identity requires significant departure from Material aesthetics
Minimal or brutalist design direction
Projects where dynamic color adds unwanted complexity
signals: Android-first or Android-primary product
Flutter or Jetpack Compose codebase
Requirement for wallpaper-based dynamic theming
Cross-platform product needing unified design language
key_concepts: Dynamic color: tonal palettes extracted from user content
Color roles: primary, secondary, tertiary, error, surface, outline
Shape scale: none, extra-small, small, medium, large, extra-large, full
Type scale: display, headline, title, body, label in large/medium/small
Elevation: tonal color overlay replacing drop shadows
Component tokens: each component has a defined token contract

systems.name: Apple Human Interface Guidelines (HIG)
systems.description: Apple's design guidelines for iOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS, and visionOS. Emphasizes clarity, deference to content, depth through layering, and platform-specific conventions. The gold standard for native Apple platform experiences.
principles: Clarity: text is legible, icons precise, adornments subtle and purposeful
Deference: fluid motion and crisp interface help users understand content
Depth: visual layers and realistic motion convey hierarchy and vitality
Direct manipulation: users feel connected to content through gestures
Consistency: familiar patterns across the Apple ecosystem
when_to_use: Native iOS or macOS application development
SwiftUI or UIKit projects targeting Apple platforms
Apps intended for App Store approval
visionOS spatial computing interfaces
avoid_when: Cross-platform product where platform consistency matters more
Android-primary applications
Web applications not embedded in Apple platforms
Brand experience that intentionally diverges from platform norms
signals: Swift or Objective-C codebase
Product targets iPhone, iPad, Mac, or Apple Watch
App Store distribution planned
Users expect native platform behavior
key_concepts: SF Symbols: 5000+ consistent iconography symbols
Dynamic Type: system-wide text size preferences
Safe Area: respecting notches, home indicator, status bar
Navigation patterns: tab bar, navigation stack, modal presentation
Haptic feedback: taptic engine integration patterns
Live Activities and widgets: glanceable information design

systems.name: Ant Design
systems.description: Enterprise-level UI design language and React UI library from Ant Group. Focuses on practical, efficient interfaces for data-heavy enterprise applications with comprehensive component coverage and strong i18n support.
principles: Natural: visual behavior reflects real-world interactions
Certain: provide clear, predictable design patterns
Meaningful: purposeful design with clear information hierarchy
Growing: design that evolves and improves continuously
when_to_use: Enterprise dashboards and admin panels
Data-heavy applications with complex forms and tables
React applications needing comprehensive component library
Internal tools requiring rapid development
avoid_when: Consumer-facing product requiring unique brand identity
Minimalist design direction with few components
Non-React technology stack
Mobile-first applications requiring native feel
signals: React codebase for enterprise application
Complex data tables, forms, and filters
Admin panel or back-office tool
Chinese market or international enterprise product
key_concepts: Design values: natural, certain, meaningful, growing
Token system: seed tokens, map tokens, alias tokens
ProComponents: high-level business components
ConfigProvider: global theme and locale configuration
Dense mode: compact layouts for data-heavy interfaces

systems.name: Carbon Design System
systems.description: IBM's open-source design system built for products and digital experiences. Features a strong 2x grid, comprehensive accessibility support, and a token-based theme system. Designed for complex enterprise software at scale.
principles: Be essential: every element serves a purpose
Be inclusive: accessible to all users regardless of ability
Be consistent: unified experience across IBM products
Be humanistic: design for real people in real contexts
when_to_use: IBM ecosystem products or integrations
Enterprise applications requiring strong accessibility
Products needing comprehensive grid and layout system
React or web-components based projects
avoid_when: Consumer-facing product needing playful aesthetics
Small projects where Carbon's weight is excessive
Non-web platforms without Carbon support
Brand identity conflicts with IBM's visual language
signals: IBM product or partner integration
Strong accessibility compliance requirements
Complex data visualization needs
Enterprise React application
key_concepts: 2x grid: everything aligns to 2px mini-unit grid
Themes: white, g10, g90, g100 with token mappings
Shell: common application frame with header and side nav
Data visualization: comprehensive charting guidelines
Motion: productive and expressive motion categories

systems.name: Fluent Design System
systems.description: Microsoft's cross-platform design system for Windows, web, iOS, and Android. Features Fluent UI components, a depth system using Acrylic and Reveal effects, and adaptive design for diverse input modes including touch, pen, keyboard, and mouse.
principles: Light: use light to guide attention and convey hierarchy
Depth: create layers that establish hierarchy and focus
Motion: connect experiences with purposeful animation
Material: use textures and effects that feel tangible
Scale: adapt to every screen size and input mode
when_to_use: Microsoft ecosystem applications (Teams, Office, Windows)
Cross-platform apps requiring consistent Microsoft experience
Enterprise products targeting Windows-heavy user base
React-based web applications needing corporate design system
avoid_when: Apple or Google ecosystem-primary products
Products requiring minimal, content-first aesthetic
Small projects where Fluent's complexity is overkill
Highly branded consumer products
signals: Microsoft 365 or Teams integration
Windows-primary desktop application
React codebase targeting enterprise users
Multi-input modality requirements (touch, pen, mouse)
key_concepts: Acrylic: translucent material effect for depth
Reveal: highlight effect showing interactive elements
Connected animations: shared element transitions between views
Responsive breakpoints: small, medium, large, extra-large
Fluent UI React: comprehensive component library
Tokens: design tokens for cross-platform consistency

systems.name: Adobe Spectrum
systems.description: Adobe's design system powering Creative Cloud, Document Cloud, and Experience Cloud products. Built for complex creative and enterprise tools with emphasis on precision, information density, and cross-platform consistency.
principles: Rational: logical, consistent foundation for every decision
Human: warm, approachable, and considerate of diverse needs
Focused: direct attention to what matters most
Unified: coherent experience across all Adobe products
when_to_use: Adobe ecosystem integrations or plugins
Complex creative tool interfaces
High information density applications
Products requiring precise control interfaces (sliders, color pickers)
avoid_when: Simple consumer applications
Products with minimal UI surface area
Non-web platforms without Spectrum support
Highly playful or casual brand identities
signals: Adobe product integration or plugin development
Complex creative workflows with toolbars and panels
High information density with many controls
Web components or React-based creative tools
key_concepts: Spectrum CSS: skin-based component styling
Spectrum Web Components: lit-based component library
Scale: medium and large UI density modes
Color themes: lightest, light, dark, darkest
Express theme: more colorful variant for creative tools

systems.name: Salesforce Lightning Design System
systems.description: Salesforce's design system for building enterprise CRM interfaces. Provides comprehensive patterns for data tables, record layouts, and complex form workflows. Includes styling hooks (CSS custom properties) for controlled customization.
principles: Clarity: eliminate ambiguity with clear visual hierarchy
Efficiency: streamline repetitive tasks with smart defaults
Consistency: unified experience across Salesforce ecosystem
Beauty: professional polish that builds trust
when_to_use: Salesforce platform development (Lightning, LWC)
CRM and sales workflow interfaces
Enterprise applications with record-based layouts
Data-heavy applications with complex table interactions
avoid_when: Consumer-facing products outside Salesforce ecosystem
Simple marketing sites or landing pages
Products requiring distinctive brand identity
Non-enterprise contexts where Lightning looks heavy
signals: Salesforce Lightning Web Components (LWC) codebase
CRM-style record detail and list views
Enterprise sales or service workflow
Apex and Visualforce integration
key_concepts: Blueprints: design patterns for common enterprise scenarios
Styling hooks: CSS custom properties for safe customization
Lightning Web Components: standards-based component model
Record layouts: compound, related lists, detail panels
Utility bar: persistent footer with quick actions

systems.name: Shopify Polaris
systems.description: Shopify's design system for building merchant-facing admin experiences. Optimized for commerce workflows including product management, order processing, and analytics. Features strong mobile patterns and a token-based theme system.
principles: Put merchants first: every decision serves the merchant
Empower but don't overwhelm: progressive disclosure of complexity
Be consistent but not rigid: allow contextual adaptation
Optimize for clarity: unambiguous labels, actions, and feedback
when_to_use: Shopify app or integration development
Commerce and merchant dashboard interfaces
Admin panels with product/order/customer management
React-based e-commerce applications
avoid_when: Consumer-facing storefront design
Non-commerce applications
Products with no relation to Shopify ecosystem
Highly creative or editorial design directions
signals: Shopify app or plugin development
Commerce admin interface with product management
Merchant-facing dashboard or analytics
React codebase targeting e-commerce
key_concepts: Resource pages: index, detail, create, edit patterns
Action patterns: primary, secondary, destructive actions
App Bridge: embedding in Shopify admin
Token system: color, spacing, typography, shape tokens
Patterns: error handling, loading states, empty states

systems.name: GitHub Primer
systems.description: GitHub's design system providing components, patterns, and guidelines for building GitHub's UI. Features a robust token system, strong accessibility focus, and components available in React, Rails ViewComponents, and CSS.
principles: Responsive: works across all screen sizes
Accessible: meets WCAG 2.1 AA standards
Themeable: supports light, dark, and high contrast modes
Consistent: unified patterns across GitHub's surface area
when_to_use: GitHub integrations or extensions
Developer-facing tools and dashboards
Code-centric interfaces with diffs, files, and repos
React or Rails applications needing developer-oriented UI
avoid_when: Consumer products unrelated to development
Highly branded marketing experiences
Mobile-native applications
Enterprise CRM or business workflow tools
signals: GitHub App or Action development
Developer tool with code-centric UI
React or Rails ViewComponent codebase
Interface with repository, issue, or PR workflows
key_concepts: Primer React: React component library
Primer ViewComponents: Rails server-rendered components
Primer CSS: utility-first CSS framework
Color modes: light, dark, dark dimmed, high contrast
ActionList: flexible list pattern for menus, navigation, selections

systems.name: Chakra UI Component Philosophy
systems.description: Chakra UI's approach to building accessible React components with style props. Emphasizes composability through granular component APIs, built-in accessibility, and a theme-driven style system using CSS-in-JS.
principles: Style props: apply styles directly as component props
Composable: combine small components to build complex UIs
Accessible: WAI-ARIA compliant out of the box
Themeable: comprehensive theme object for global customization
Developer experience: intuitive API with TypeScript support
when_to_use: React projects needing quick accessible component setup
Teams preferring style props over CSS-in-JS or utility classes
Projects requiring strong theming with minimal configuration
Rapid prototyping with production-quality accessibility
avoid_when: Performance-critical applications where CSS-in-JS overhead matters
Projects requiring zero-runtime CSS solution
Server-side rendering with strict CSS extraction needs
Non-React technology stacks
signals: React codebase with emphasis on accessibility
Team prefers component-level styling over separate CSS
Need for rapid UI development with consistent design
TypeScript project wanting typed style props
key_concepts: Style props: <Box p={4} bg='blue.500' />
Theme object: colors, fonts, sizes, breakpoints, components
Component variants: size, variant, colorScheme props
useColorMode: light/dark mode hook
Compound components: Menu, Tabs, Accordion patterns

systems.name: Radix UI Headless Component Architecture
systems.description: Radix's approach to building unstyled, accessible UI primitives. Provides behavior, accessibility, and keyboard interactions without any visual styling. Designed to be composed with any styling solution.
principles: Headless: zero visual styling, complete behavior implementation
Accessible: full WAI-ARIA compliance and keyboard navigation
Composable: granular part-based API for custom composition
Uncontrolled by default: works without state management, controllable when needed
Developer ownership: full control over markup and styling
when_to_use: Custom design system requiring accessible primitives
Projects needing complete visual control with guaranteed accessibility
Building branded components that don't look like any design system
Pairing with Tailwind CSS or CSS Modules for styling
avoid_when: Team needs pre-styled components for rapid development
Project lacks design resources to style all components
Quick prototype where visual polish is secondary
Non-React projects
signals: Custom design system build in progress
Team has designers creating bespoke component designs
Accessibility compliance is a hard requirement
Tailwind CSS or CSS Modules in the codebase
key_concepts: Part-based API: <Dialog.Root>, <Dialog.Trigger>, <Dialog.Content>
Polymorphic asChild: render as any element with <Slot>
Animation support: data-state attributes for enter/exit animations
Collision-aware positioning: popper-based floating elements
Controlled/uncontrolled: value and defaultValue patterns

systems.name: shadcn/ui Copy-Paste Component Philosophy
systems.description: A collection of re-usable React components built on Radix UI primitives and styled with Tailwind CSS. Unlike traditional component libraries, code is copied into your project rather than installed as a dependency, giving full ownership and customization control.
principles: Own your code: components live in your codebase, not node_modules
Copy-paste over dependency: no version lock-in or breaking changes
Tailwind-native: utility classes as the styling primitive
Radix-powered: headless accessibility from Radix primitives
Composable: components are building blocks, not black boxes
when_to_use: Next.js or React projects using Tailwind CSS
Teams wanting full control over component code
Projects where dependency updates cause friction
Building custom design system on top of accessible primitives
avoid_when: Team prefers managed dependency updates
Project does not use Tailwind CSS
Need for automatic security patches in components
Large team where component code divergence is a risk
signals: Next.js + Tailwind CSS project
components.json file in project root
components/ui directory with copied component files
cn() utility function for className merging
key_concepts: CLI: npx shadcn-ui@latest add [component]
cn() utility: clsx + tailwind-merge for className composition
components.json: project configuration for paths and styling
Variants: class-variance-authority (cva) for component variants
Registry: browsable component catalog with preview

systems.name: Tailwind CSS Utility-First Methodology
systems.description: Utility-first CSS framework where designs are built by composing small, single-purpose utility classes directly in HTML markup. Eliminates custom CSS by providing a comprehensive set of constrained design tokens as classes.
principles: Utility-first: compose designs from atomic utility classes
Constrained design: predefined scales prevent arbitrary values
Responsive: mobile-first breakpoint prefixes (sm:, md:, lg:)
State variants: hover:, focus:, dark: modifiers on any utility
Just-in-time: generate only the CSS you actually use
when_to_use: Rapid UI development with consistent spacing and color
Projects where developers own the styling process
Component-based frameworks (React, Vue, Svelte)
Teams wanting to avoid CSS naming conventions and specificity
avoid_when: Team strongly prefers semantic CSS or BEM methodology
Large legacy codebase with established CSS architecture
Projects requiring dynamic runtime style changes
Content-heavy sites where prose styling needs are dominant
signals: tailwind.config.js or tailwind.config.ts in project root
Class strings with patterns like p-4 text-lg bg-blue-500
PostCSS or Vite with Tailwind plugin configured
@apply directives in CSS files
key_concepts: Color palette: 50-950 shades for each color
Responsive prefixes: sm:, md:, lg:, xl:, 2xl:
Arbitrary values: w-[137px] for one-off values
Plugins: extend with custom utilities and components
@layer: base, components, utilities for organization

systems.name: Color Theory for Interface Design
systems.description: Foundational principles of color including the color wheel, color harmony models, color psychology, and accessibility requirements. Provides the scientific and perceptual basis for all color decisions in digital interfaces.
principles: Color communicates meaning, mood, and hierarchy
Harmony models create visually pleasing combinations
Context changes color perception (simultaneous contrast)
Accessibility requires sufficient contrast ratios
Cultural associations with color vary globally
when_to_use: Establishing brand color palette for a product
Evaluating whether color choices are harmonious
Diagnosing readability or accessibility issues
Creating data visualization color scales
avoid_when: Color palette is already established and validated
Working within a design system with fixed color tokens
Grayscale-first design approach is more appropriate
signals: Colors feel random or clash visually
Brand colors fail accessibility contrast checks
Data visualization colors are indistinguishable
Color palette does not scale to dark mode
key_concepts: Color wheel: primary, secondary, tertiary colors
Complementary: opposite on the color wheel, high contrast
Analogous: adjacent on the wheel, harmonious
Triadic: three equidistant colors, vibrant
Split-complementary: base + two adjacent to complement
HSL model: hue, saturation, lightness for digital color
OKLCH: perceptually uniform color space for modern CSS
Color blindness: deuteranopia, protanopia, tritanopia considerations

systems.name: Typographic Scales and Hierarchies
systems.description: Systems for establishing harmonious type size relationships using mathematical ratios. Modular scales create consistent visual rhythm and clear information hierarchy across headings, body text, captions, and labels.
principles: Use a mathematical ratio for consistent size progression
Fewer sizes create cleaner hierarchy
Line height decreases proportionally as font size increases
Font weight and size work together for hierarchy
Responsive scales may need different ratios per breakpoint
when_to_use: Establishing type system for a new design system
Auditing inconsistent font sizes in existing product
Creating responsive typography that scales smoothly
Setting up design tokens for type sizes
avoid_when: Using a design system with pre-established type scale
Single-size text interfaces (terminal, code editor)
Experimental typography where mathematical harmony is undesirable
signals: Font sizes in codebase are arbitrary px values
No consistent ratio between heading levels
Typography tokens are missing or inconsistent
Type hierarchy is flat and lacks visual distinction
key_concepts: Base size: typically 16px for body text
Scale ratio: multiplier between each step
Minor second (1.067), Major second (1.125)
Minor third (1.200), Major third (1.250)
Perfect fourth (1.333), Augmented fourth (1.414)
Perfect fifth (1.500), Golden ratio (1.618)
Steps: typically 6-10 sizes in a complete scale

systems.name: Spacing Systems and Spatial Rhythm
systems.description: Systematic approaches to spacing using base-unit grids (4px or 8px). Creates consistent padding, margins, and gaps throughout an interface. Spatial rhythm improves scanability and reduces visual noise.
principles: Use a base unit (4px or 8px) for all spacing values
Larger spacing separates unrelated groups
Smaller spacing connects related elements
Consistent spacing reduces cognitive load
Spacing scale should have enough steps for flexibility
when_to_use: Establishing spacing tokens for a design system
Auditing inconsistent spacing in existing product
Aligning elements to a consistent spatial grid
Creating Tailwind or token-based spacing scale
avoid_when: Organic or editorial layouts where rigid grids are undesirable
Pixel-art or retro interfaces with non-standard spacing
Already using a design system with established spacing
signals: Spacing values in CSS are arbitrary and inconsistent
Elements feel randomly placed without visual rhythm
Padding and margins differ between similar components
Developers are guessing spacing values
key_concepts: T-shirt sizes: xs(4), sm(8), md(16), lg(24), xl(32), 2xl(48)
Component spacing: internal padding vs external margin
Stack spacing: consistent vertical rhythm between sections
Inline spacing: horizontal rhythm between inline elements

systems.name: Elevation and Visual Depth System
systems.description: System for conveying hierarchy and interactivity through shadows, z-index layering, and surface tinting. Elevation communicates which elements sit above others, which are interactive, and how content is organized in layers.
principles: Higher elevation indicates greater importance or interactivity
Elevation levels should be distinct and limited
Shadows simulate real-world light sources consistently
Dark mode may use surface tinting instead of shadows
Z-index should follow a managed scale, not arbitrary values
when_to_use: Designing overlays, modals, dropdowns, and tooltips
Creating visual hierarchy between surface layers
Establishing a z-index management system
Dark mode implementation where shadows are less visible
avoid_when: Flat design direction with no depth cues
Interfaces with minimal layering (single-surface apps)
Print or static design where depth is irrelevant
signals: Z-index values are arbitrary and conflicting
Shadows are inconsistent across components
Modal and dropdown layering causes rendering issues
Dark mode shadows are invisible or muddy
key_concepts: Elevation levels: 0 (flat), 1 (raised), 2 (overlay), 3 (modal), 4 (toast)
Shadow tokens: offset-x, offset-y, blur, spread, color per level
Z-index scale: base(0), dropdown(1000), sticky(1100), modal(1300), toast(1400)
Surface tinting: lighter surface = higher elevation in dark mode
Ring/outline: alternative to shadow for subtle elevation

systems.name: Iconography Design Principles
systems.description: Principles for designing, selecting, and implementing icons in digital interfaces. Covers icon grids, optical sizing, stroke consistency, accessibility labeling, and icon system management.
principles: Icons supplement text; they rarely replace it for critical actions
Consistent stroke weight, corner radius, and grid across all icons
Optical sizing ensures icons look correct at different scales
Every interactive icon needs an accessible label
Decorative icons should be hidden from screen readers
when_to_use: Building or selecting an icon set for a product
Auditing icon consistency across an interface
Implementing icon accessibility (aria-label, role)
Creating icon component with size and color variants
avoid_when: Text-only interface where icons add noise
Icon meaning is ambiguous without established convention
Using icons as the sole means of communication
signals: Icons from multiple sets with inconsistent style
Interactive icons missing accessible labels
Icons rendered at inconsistent sizes
SVG icons with hardcoded colors that don't theme
key_concepts: Icon grid: 24x24px with 2px padding, 20x20 live area
Stroke weight: 1.5px or 2px consistent across set
Filled vs outlined: outlined for unselected, filled for selected
Optical sizing: adjust weight and detail for small vs large sizes
SVG sprite: inline SVG with currentColor for theming
Icon fonts vs inline SVG: SVG preferred for accessibility

systems.name: Illustration System and Style Guidelines
systems.description: Framework for maintaining consistent illustration style, subject matter, and usage across a product. Covers style definition, composition rules, color palette constraints, and guidelines for when to use illustration vs photography vs iconography.
principles: Illustrations reinforce brand personality and tone
Consistent style across all illustrations (stroke, color, proportion)
Illustrations should clarify, not decorate
Accessible: illustrations need alt text describing their content
Cultural sensitivity in representation of people and places
when_to_use: Defining illustration style for a brand or product
Empty states, onboarding, and error pages
Marketing and landing page visual storytelling
Creating illustration guidelines for multiple artists
avoid_when: Product has no illustration needs (dev tools, terminals)
Photography is more appropriate for the content type
Budget does not allow for custom illustration creation
Illustrations would slow page load without adding value
signals: Illustrations from different sources lack visual cohesion
Empty states use generic stock illustrations
No style guide exists for illustration contributors
Illustrations are decorative with no informational value
key_concepts: Style definition: line weight, color palette, perspective, shading
Spot illustrations: small, focused, single-concept (empty states)
Hero illustrations: large, complex, narrative (landing pages)
Character design: consistent proportions and style for people
Illustration tokens: constrained color palette matching design system
SVG optimization: SVGO for production illustration assets

systems.name: W3C Design Tokens Specification
systems.description: The W3C Design Tokens Community Group specification for a standard file format to exchange design tokens between tools and platforms. Defines how to represent colors, dimensions, typography, shadows, and other design decisions as platform-agnostic data.
principles: Tokens are the single source of truth for design decisions
Platform-agnostic: one definition, multiple output formats
Hierarchical: organize tokens in nested groups
Typed: each token has a defined type (color, dimension, etc.)
Aliasable: tokens can reference other tokens
when_to_use: Building a design system that spans multiple platforms
Syncing design tools (Figma) with code implementation
Creating a theme system with swappable token sets
Establishing a single source of truth for design decisions
avoid_when: Single-platform project with simple styling needs
Prototype or throwaway project
Team has no design system infrastructure
Design decisions are still highly volatile
signals: Hardcoded color values scattered across CSS files
Design-to-code handoff loses fidelity
Multiple platforms (web, iOS, Android) with inconsistent values
Theme switching requires touching many files
key_concepts: Token types: color, dimension, fontFamily, fontWeight, duration, cubicBezier
Groups: hierarchical namespacing for organization
Aliases: {color.primary.500} references another token
Composite tokens: typography, shadow, border as structured values
Style Dictionary: build system for transforming tokens to platform code
DTCG format: $value, $type, $description fields
systems.origin: Salesforce / W3C Design Tokens Community Group

systems.name: Semantic Token Architecture
systems.description: Layer of alias tokens that map meaningful names to primitive values. Instead of using color.blue.500, semantic tokens use color.action.primary or color.text.secondary. This abstraction enables theming and ensures tokens communicate intent rather than appearance.
principles: Token names describe purpose, not appearance
Semantic tokens alias primitive tokens, never hardcoded values
Enable theme switching by remapping semantic to primitive
Reduce decision fatigue: developers choose by intent
Semantic layer is the public API of the token system
when_to_use: Design system needs to support multiple themes
Developers are choosing wrong primitive tokens for components
Dark mode requires different primitives for same semantic purpose
Token names like blue-500 are used where intent matters
avoid_when: Single-theme application with no foreseeable theme changes
Very small project where token abstraction adds overhead
Primitive palette is not yet established
signals: Primitive color names used directly in component styles
Dark mode implementation requires finding every color usage
Developers unsure which shade of gray to use for text
New theme requires touching every component file
key_concepts: Primitive tokens: color.blue.500, space.4, font.size.md
Semantic tokens: color.bg.primary, color.text.secondary, space.gap.md
Mapping: color.bg.primary -> color.white (light) or color.gray.900 (dark)
Intent categories: action, feedback, surface, border, text
State modifiers: default, hover, active, disabled, focus

systems.name: Component-Level Token Contracts
systems.description: Per-component token definitions that create a stable API between design and engineering. Each component declares its own tokens (button.bg, button.text, button.border) that map to semantic tokens, enabling component-level theming without global side effects.
principles: Each component has an explicit token contract
Component tokens reference semantic tokens, not primitives
Token contract is the component's styling API
Changes to component tokens don't leak to other components
Variants (size, state) are expressed through token overrides
when_to_use: Building a component library with per-component theming
Component customization needs to be safe and contained
Design handoff requires precise component-level specifications
White-label product where components are themed per customer
avoid_when: Utility-first CSS approach (Tailwind) is the styling method
Small project with few custom components
Token system is not yet established at semantic level
Components are simple enough to style inline
signals: Components use global CSS variables causing unintended side effects
Theming a single component requires understanding the whole token tree
Component styling is tightly coupled to implementation details
No clear contract between design specs and component code
key_concepts: Contract: button.bg.default, button.bg.hover, button.text, button.border
Mapping: button.bg.default -> color.action.primary -> color.blue.600
Variants: button.bg.default.secondary -> color.action.secondary
States: default, hover, active, focus, disabled per token
CSS custom properties: --button-bg as the component API

systems.name: Multi-Theme Token Architecture
systems.description: Architecture for supporting multiple themes (light, dark, high contrast, brand variants) through token set swapping. The semantic layer remains constant while the underlying primitive mappings change per theme, enabling runtime theme switching.
principles: Themes swap primitive mappings, not semantic names
All themes must satisfy the same semantic token contract
Theme tokens are applied via CSS custom properties or context
High contrast theme is an accessibility requirement, not optional
Theme preference should respect system settings
when_to_use: Product requires light and dark mode support
White-label or multi-brand product
Accessibility compliance requires high contrast mode
User preference for theme selection is a feature
avoid_when: Single fixed theme with no planned variation
Prototype or MVP where theming is premature
Static marketing site with fixed brand colors
signals: Dark mode is planned or requested by users
Multiple brands or tenants need different visual identity
prefers-color-scheme media query is not being respected
Theme switching causes flash of unstyled content
key_concepts: Theme sets: light, dark, high-contrast, brand-a, brand-b
Token override: same semantic name, different primitive value per theme
CSS custom properties: applied on :root or [data-theme] selector
System preference: prefers-color-scheme and prefers-contrast
Theme persistence: localStorage or server-side cookie
Flash prevention: blocking script or server-rendered theme class

systems.name: Grid Systems for Interface Layout
systems.description: Structured layout systems using columns, gutters, and margins to organize content. Includes column grids for page layout, baseline grids for vertical rhythm, and modular grids combining both. Provides structure and consistency across pages and screen sizes.
principles: Grids provide structure, not constraint
Column count adapts to screen width
Gutters maintain consistent spacing between columns
Margins define the relationship between content and screen edge
Content alignment to grid creates visual order
when_to_use: Establishing page-level layout structure
Multi-column content layouts (dashboards, feeds, catalogs)
Responsive layouts needing consistent column behavior
Design systems requiring standardized layout patterns
avoid_when: Single-column mobile interface
Freeform canvas or spatial interfaces
Full-bleed media-heavy layouts
Simple stacked content layouts
signals: Content alignment is inconsistent across pages
Layout breaks at certain screen sizes
Developers are creating custom grid solutions per page
Design specs show different column structures per page
key_concepts: Column grid: 4 (mobile), 8 (tablet), 12 (desktop) columns
Gutters: space between columns (16px, 24px, 32px typical)
Margins: space between grid and viewport edge
Baseline grid: horizontal lines for vertical rhythm (4px or 8px)
Modular grid: intersection of column grid and baseline grid
Subgrid: CSS subgrid for nested grid alignment

systems.name: Responsive Design Methodology
systems.description: Mobile-first approach to designing interfaces that adapt fluidly across screen sizes. Uses breakpoints, fluid units, and flexible layouts to provide optimal experiences from phone to ultrawide desktop without separate implementations.
principles: Mobile-first: design for smallest screen, enhance for larger
Content determines breakpoints, not device dimensions
Fluid layouts between breakpoints, not just at breakpoints
Progressive enhancement over graceful degradation
Touch targets: minimum 44x44px for mobile interactions
when_to_use: Any web application or site accessed across devices
Products where mobile and desktop share the same codebase
Content-driven sites needing readability at all sizes
E-commerce where mobile conversion is critical
avoid_when: Native mobile app with platform-specific layouts
Desktop-only enterprise tool with no mobile requirement
Fixed-dimension embedded widgets or kiosk applications
Email templates where responsive support is limited
signals: Layout breaks or overflows at certain viewport widths
Touch targets are too small on mobile
Content is hidden on mobile instead of reorganized
Separate mobile site exists (m.example.com)
key_concepts: Breakpoints: 640px (sm), 768px (md), 1024px (lg), 1280px (xl), 1536px (2xl)
Fluid units: %, vw, vh, rem, em for proportional sizing
clamp(): min, preferred, max for fluid values
Media queries: min-width for mobile-first, max-width for desktop-first
Viewport meta: <meta name='viewport' content='width=device-width'>
Responsive images: srcset, sizes, picture element

systems.name: Container Queries for Component-Level Responsiveness
systems.description: CSS container queries enable components to respond to their container's dimensions rather than the viewport. This allows truly reusable components that adapt their layout based on available space regardless of where they are placed in the page.
principles: Components should respond to their container, not the viewport
Container queries enable context-independent component design
Containment context must be explicitly declared
Container query units (cqw, cqh) enable fluid internal sizing
Combine with media queries for page-level responsive decisions
when_to_use: Components used in varying layout contexts (sidebar vs main content)
Design system components needing context-independent responsiveness
Card layouts that adapt from horizontal to vertical based on space
Dashboard widgets placed in different grid configurations
avoid_when: Page-level layout decisions (use media queries)
Browser support requirements exclude container query support
Simple components that don't need responsive behavior
Components always rendered in the same context
signals: Components break when moved to a narrower container
Media queries on components assume a specific page layout
Responsive behavior is tied to viewport rather than available space
Same component needs different breakpoints in different locations
key_concepts: container-type: inline-size for width-based queries
@container (min-width: 400px) { } for container-scoped styles
container-name: for named containment contexts
Container query units: cqw, cqh, cqi, cqb
Style queries: @container style(--theme: dark) for custom property queries
Containment: size, layout, style containment types

systems.name: Flexbox Layout Patterns
systems.description: Common layout patterns achievable with CSS Flexbox. Covers one-dimensional distribution of space, alignment, ordering, and wrapping. The go-to tool for component-level layout including navigation bars, card rows, form layouts, and centering.
principles: Flexbox is for one-dimensional layout (row OR column)
Use flex-grow, flex-shrink, flex-basis for size distribution
Alignment: justify-content for main axis, align-items for cross axis
Order property enables visual reordering without DOM changes
gap property replaces margin hacks for consistent spacing
when_to_use: Horizontal navigation bars and toolbars
Centering content vertically and horizontally
Distributing space between items in a row or column
Card layouts with equal-height columns
avoid_when: Two-dimensional grid layout is needed (use CSS Grid)
Complex page-level layout with rows and columns
Layout requires explicit row/column track sizing
Subgrid alignment is needed across nested containers
signals: Floating or inline-block used for layout instead of flex
Vertical centering achieved with padding hacks
Margins used for spacing between flex children
Equal-height columns failing without explicit height
key_concepts: display: flex and flex-direction: row | column
justify-content: flex-start, center, space-between, space-around, space-evenly
align-items: stretch, center, flex-start, flex-end, baseline
flex: grow shrink basis shorthand
flex-wrap: wrap for multi-line flex containers
gap: row-gap and column-gap for consistent spacing

systems.name: CSS Grid Layout Patterns
systems.description: Two-dimensional layout patterns using CSS Grid. Enables complex page layouts, dashboard grids, and responsive card grids with precise control over both rows and columns. Supports named areas, auto-placement, and subgrid for nested alignment.
principles: CSS Grid is for two-dimensional layout (rows AND columns)
Define explicit tracks or use auto-fill/auto-fit for responsive grids
Named grid areas create readable, maintainable layout definitions
Subgrid enables nested grids to align with parent tracks
minmax() enables flexible yet constrained track sizing
when_to_use: Page-level layout with header, sidebar, main, footer
Responsive card grids that reflow based on available space
Dashboard layouts with varying widget sizes
Overlapping elements without absolute positioning
avoid_when: Simple one-dimensional alignment (use Flexbox)
Linear content flow that wraps naturally
Layout is purely sequential with no grid structure
Very old browser support is required
signals: Complex layout achieved with nested flexbox containers
Absolute positioning used for layout instead of stacking context
Float-based grid system still in use
Card grids not reflowing smoothly at different widths
key_concepts: grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(250px, 1fr))
grid-template-areas: named regions for semantic layout
fr unit: fractional unit for proportional track sizing
minmax(): constrained flexible sizing
auto-fill vs auto-fit: fill vs collapse empty tracks
subgrid: inherit parent grid tracks in nested grid
grid-auto-flow: dense for gap-filling placement

systems.name: Holy Grail Application Shell Layout
systems.description: The classic application shell pattern with a fixed header, optional sidebar navigation, scrollable main content area, and footer. The foundational layout for dashboards, admin panels, and content management systems.
principles: Header and footer are fixed or sticky, content scrolls
Sidebar collapses responsively on smaller screens
Main content area takes remaining available space
Layout should work with varying content lengths
Navigation structure visible at all times on desktop
when_to_use: Dashboard or admin panel interfaces
Content management systems with navigation
Email clients, project management tools, documentation sites
Any application needing persistent navigation with content area
avoid_when: Marketing or landing page layouts
Mobile-only applications using tab navigation
Full-screen immersive experiences (games, presentations)
Simple single-column content pages
signals: Application needs persistent top and side navigation
Content area should scroll independently from navigation
Sidebar needs to toggle between expanded and collapsed
Layout structure is consistent across all pages
key_concepts: CSS Grid: grid-template-areas for header, sidebar, main, footer
Sticky header: position: sticky; top: 0 with z-index
Collapsible sidebar: width transition with toggle button
Content scroll: overflow-y: auto on main content area
Full viewport: min-height: 100vh or 100dvh for mobile
Responsive: sidebar becomes bottom drawer or hamburger menu on mobile

systems.name: Motion Design Principles for UI
systems.description: Disney's 12 principles of animation adapted for user interface design. Provides guidelines for creating motion that feels natural, communicates meaning, and enhances rather than distracts from the user experience.
principles: Motion should have clear purpose: feedback, orientation, or delight
Duration should match the size and importance of the change
Objects should move along natural, curved paths
Motion should respect user preferences (prefers-reduced-motion)
Choreography: related elements should move in coordinated sequence
when_to_use: Adding transitions between UI states
Providing feedback for user interactions
Guiding attention to important changes
Creating spatial relationships between views
avoid_when: User has prefers-reduced-motion enabled
Motion would delay task completion
Animation is purely decorative with no functional purpose
Performance budget does not allow for animation
signals: UI state changes feel abrupt or jarring
Users lose context during navigation transitions
Animations feel robotic or mechanical
Motion is inconsistent in duration and style across the product
key_concepts: Squash and stretch: subtle scale on press/release
Anticipation: brief wind-up before action
Follow-through: elements settle after main motion
Staging: direct attention to the most important action
Ease-in: accelerating (exiting elements)
Ease-out: decelerating (entering elements)
Duration scale: 100ms micro, 200ms small, 300ms medium, 500ms large
Reduce motion: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)

systems.name: Easing Curves and Timing Functions
systems.description: Mathematical curves that control the acceleration and deceleration of animations. The easing function determines whether motion feels mechanical (linear), natural (ease), snappy (ease-out), or energetic (spring). Critical for motion that feels right.
principles: Linear motion feels mechanical and should be rare
Ease-out (decelerate) for elements entering the screen
Ease-in (accelerate) for elements exiting the screen
Ease-in-out for elements that stay on screen but change state
Spring curves feel most natural for interactive elements
when_to_use: Defining animation timing for a design system
Transitions between UI states (open/close, show/hide)
Interactive elements responding to user input
Scroll-linked animations and parallax effects
avoid_when: Animation duration is too short for easing to be perceived (<50ms)
Continuous looping animations where linear is appropriate
Progress indicators where linear represents actual progress
signals: Animations feel robotic or unnatural
Transitions have inconsistent timing across the product
Custom cubic-bezier values without design rationale
Spring animations feel bouncy or sluggish
key_concepts: Linear: cubic-bezier(0, 0, 1, 1) — constant speed
Ease: cubic-bezier(0.25, 0.1, 0.25, 1) — CSS default
Ease-in: cubic-bezier(0.42, 0, 1, 1) — slow start, fast end
Ease-out: cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.58, 1) — fast start, slow end
Ease-in-out: cubic-bezier(0.42, 0, 0.58, 1) — slow start and end
Spring: defined by mass, stiffness, damping (not cubic-bezier)
CSS: transition-timing-function and animation-timing-function
Web Animations API: easing property on KeyframeEffect

systems.name: Enter/Exit/Shared Element Transitions
systems.description: Patterns for animating elements entering the DOM, leaving the DOM, and transitioning between views with shared elements. Includes the View Transitions API for cross-document transitions and techniques for maintaining spatial context during navigation.
principles: Enter: fade + scale up or slide from direction of origin
Exit: fade + scale down or slide toward destination
Shared elements: morph between views to maintain context
Exit happens before or simultaneously with enter
Duration should scale with the size of the transition
when_to_use: Modal and dialog open/close animations
Page or view navigation transitions
List item additions and removals
Expandable cards or detail views
avoid_when: Instant feedback is more appropriate (toggle switches)
User has prefers-reduced-motion enabled
Transition would block user interaction
Content is below the fold and transition is not visible
signals: Modals appear and disappear abruptly
Navigation between views feels disconnected
List items pop in without animation on data load
Users lose spatial context during navigation
key_concepts: CSS: @starting-style for entry animations
CSS: transition-behavior: allow-discrete for display/visibility
View Transitions API: document.startViewTransition()
view-transition-name: for shared element identification
AnimatePresence: React pattern for exit animations (Framer Motion)
FLIP technique: First, Last, Invert, Play for layout animations
Crossfade: simultaneous fade-out old and fade-in new

systems.name: Micro-Interactions and State Feedback
systems.description: Small, contained animations that provide feedback for user actions, communicate state changes, and add polish to interactive elements. Includes hover effects, toggle animations, loading indicators, scroll-linked effects, and success/error feedback.
principles: Every interaction should have visible feedback
Micro-interactions should be fast (100-300ms)
Feedback should match the significance of the action
Subtle is better than dramatic for frequent interactions
Loading states should communicate progress when possible
when_to_use: Button press and hover feedback
Toggle switches and checkbox animations
Form validation success/error indicators
Loading spinners, skeletons, and progress bars
Scroll-triggered reveal animations
avoid_when: Animation would be distracting during focused work
Micro-interaction fires too frequently (every keystroke)
Performance impact on low-end devices
User has requested reduced motion
signals: Buttons have no hover or active state feedback
Form submission has no loading or success indicator
Toggle switches change state without animation
Content appears abruptly on scroll
key_concepts: Hover: subtle scale, shadow, or color shift (150ms ease-out)
Active/press: slight scale-down or darkening (100ms)
Toggle: smooth position and color transition (200ms ease-in-out)
Loading: skeleton screens, shimmer effect, spinner, progress bar
Success: checkmark animation, green flash, confetti (for celebrations)
Error: shake animation, red highlight, inline error message
Scroll-linked: IntersectionObserver for scroll-triggered animations

systems.name: Animation Choreography and Sequencing
systems.description: Coordinating multiple animations to create cohesive, purposeful sequences. Includes staggered list animations, cascading reveals, and orchestrated page transitions where multiple elements animate in a deliberate order to guide attention and create visual narrative.
principles: Primary action animates first, secondary elements follow
Stagger delay should be short (30-50ms) to feel connected
Total sequence duration should not exceed 500-800ms
Direction of stagger should follow reading order or focal point
Reduce to single crossfade when prefers-reduced-motion is set
when_to_use: List or grid items loading in sequence
Page sections revealing on scroll
Dashboard widgets populating with data
Complex view transitions with multiple moving parts
avoid_when: Single element animation (no choreography needed)
User is performing rapid, repeated actions
Content needs to be immediately available
Total animation sequence would exceed 1 second
signals: Multiple elements animate simultaneously creating visual chaos
Page load animations feel random or uncoordinated
List items all appear at once instead of staggering
Dashboard feels static despite dynamic data loading
key_concepts: Stagger: animation-delay incrementing per item (30-50ms)
Cascade: parent animates, then children in sequence
Orchestration: timeline-based coordination of multiple animations
Group: related elements animate together as a unit
Sequential: one completes before next begins
Overlapping: next begins before previous completes (more fluid)
CSS: animation-delay with custom property calc(var(--i) * 50ms)

systems.name: 60-30-10 Color Distribution Rule
systems.description: Interior design rule adapted for interfaces: 60% dominant color (background/surface), 30% secondary color (cards, sections, secondary surfaces), and 10% accent color (CTAs, highlights, interactive elements). Creates visual balance and clear hierarchy.
principles: 60% dominant: neutral background that does not compete for attention
30% secondary: supporting surfaces that create visual interest
10% accent: draws attention to key actions and information
Accent color should have highest contrast against its surroundings
Ratio is approximate — intent matters more than exact measurement
when_to_use: Establishing color balance for a new design
Auditing whether a page feels visually overwhelming
Choosing how to distribute brand colors across surfaces
Teaching color usage guidelines to a team
avoid_when: Data visualization requiring many distinct colors
Highly branded splash pages where accent dominates intentionally
Dark mode where the 60% is already dark and contrast differs
Artistic or editorial layouts with intentional color dominance
signals: Interface feels monotone or lacks visual hierarchy
Accent color is overused, making nothing stand out
Background color competes with content for attention
CTA buttons blend into the surrounding UI
key_concepts: Dominant (60%): white, light gray, or dark surface in dark mode
Secondary (30%): card backgrounds, navigation, sidebars
Accent (10%): primary buttons, links, active states, badges
Neutral palette: grays for the 60% and 30% in most interfaces
Brand color: typically used as the 10% accent
Validation: squint test — can you still see the hierarchy?

systems.name: WCAG Contrast Ratio Requirements
systems.description: Web Content Accessibility Guidelines requirements for text and UI component contrast against backgrounds. WCAG 2.1 AA requires 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text. AAA requires 7:1 for normal text. APCA is the emerging successor for WCAG 3.0.
principles: All text must meet minimum contrast ratios against its background
Interactive UI components need 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors
Contrast requirements apply in all themes including dark mode
Do not rely on color alone to convey information
Test with real content, not just color swatches
when_to_use: Choosing text colors for any background
Validating design system color pairings
Auditing existing interfaces for accessibility compliance
Creating accessible data visualizations
avoid_when: Decorative elements with no informational content
Disabled state styling (reduced contrast is intentional and acceptable)
Logotypes and brand marks (exempt from WCAG)
signals: Users report difficulty reading text
Automated accessibility audit flags contrast failures
Light gray text on white backgrounds
Colored text on colored backgrounds without testing
key_concepts: AA normal text: 4.5:1 minimum contrast ratio
AA large text (18pt or 14pt bold): 3:1 minimum
AA UI components: 3:1 against adjacent colors
AAA normal text: 7:1 minimum contrast ratio
AAA large text: 4.5:1 minimum
APCA (WCAG 3.0 draft): Lc values, font-size dependent thresholds
Tools: axe, Lighthouse, Stark, WebAIM contrast checker
Non-text contrast: icons, borders, focus indicators

systems.name: Dark Mode Design Patterns
systems.description: Design considerations for dark theme interfaces. Dark mode is not simply inverting colors — it requires adjusted contrast, desaturated colors, elevation through surface tinting, and careful handling of shadows, images, and illustrations.
principles: Dark gray (#121212) is better than pure black (#000000) for primary surface
Reduce saturation of colors to avoid vibration on dark backgrounds
Elevation is conveyed through lighter surface tints, not darker shadows
Text should be slightly off-white to reduce eye strain
Respect prefers-color-scheme system preference
when_to_use: Any application where users work in low-light environments
Products where dark mode is expected (code editors, media apps)
Design systems requiring theme support
Reducing eye strain for extended use sessions
avoid_when: Print-oriented document applications where WYSIWYG matters
Regulatory or legal content where light mode is standard
Applications with heavy use of photography or illustration that clashes
signals: Users requesting dark mode support
prefers-color-scheme: dark not being respected
Dark mode implementation has pure black backgrounds
Colors that look fine in light mode are garish in dark mode
key_concepts: Surface hierarchy: #121212 base, lighter tints for elevation
Material overlay: 5%, 7%, 8%, 9%, 11%, 12% white overlay per elevation level
Desaturation: reduce chroma by 20-30% for dark backgrounds
Text colors: #E0E0E0 primary, #A0A0A0 secondary, #6C6C6C disabled
prefers-color-scheme: dark media query for automatic switching
color-scheme: dark light CSS property for form controls
Image handling: reduce brightness/contrast or provide dark variants

systems.name: Semantic Color Meanings
systems.description: Standardized color associations for communicating status, feedback, and meaning in interfaces. Success (green), warning (amber/yellow), error (red), and info (blue) are universal patterns. Extends to severity levels, priority indicators, and categorical distinction.
principles: Never rely on color alone — always pair with text, icons, or patterns
Semantic colors must meet contrast requirements in all themes
Limit semantic palette to avoid color overload
Cultural color associations vary — validate for target audience
Semantic colors need light and dark variants for backgrounds vs text
when_to_use: Form validation feedback (success, error, warning)
Status indicators (active, inactive, pending)
Alert and notification banners
Data visualization with categorical meaning
avoid_when: Color meaning is ambiguous without cultural context
Using red/green for the only distinction (color blindness)
Decorative use where semantic meaning would be misleading
signals: Error messages use brand color instead of red
Success and error states are only distinguished by color
Warning banners are not visually distinct from info banners
Status indicators lack text labels
key_concepts: Success: green (#22C55E family) — completion, valid, approved
Warning: amber (#F59E0B family) — caution, attention needed
Error: red (#EF4444 family) — failure, invalid, destructive
Info: blue (#3B82F6 family) — neutral information, help
Neutral: gray (#6B7280 family) — disabled, inactive, placeholder
Background variants: light tinted backgrounds for banners (green-50, red-50)
Border variants: subtle borders matching semantic color
Pairing: icon + color + text for maximum clarity

systems.name: Major Third Type Scale (1.250)
systems.description: Typographic scale using a 1.250 ratio (major third musical interval). Produces moderate size jumps suitable for most interfaces. Starting from 16px base: 16, 20, 25, 31.25, 39, 48.8. A balanced choice between readability and hierarchy.
principles: Multiply each step by 1.250 for the next size
Round to nearest pixel or 0.25rem for clean values
Use fewer steps (6-8) for cleaner hierarchy
Pair with consistent line-height reduction at larger sizes
Consider using different scale for mobile vs desktop
when_to_use: General purpose web applications
Content-heavy sites needing moderate heading distinction
Design systems requiring a balanced type hierarchy
Projects where heading sizes should not be dramatically different
avoid_when: Dramatic visual hierarchy is needed (use larger ratio)
Dense data interfaces where size difference should be minimal
Display-heavy marketing pages needing bolder scale contrast
signals: Headings feel too similar in size to body text
Type hierarchy exists but lacks clear visual steps
Current font sizes are arbitrary without a systematic ratio
key_concepts: Ratio: 1.250 (major third)
Line height: 1.5 for body, 1.3 for subheadings, 1.1 for display
Letter spacing: tighten at larger sizes, default at body, loosen at small

systems.name: Perfect Fourth Type Scale (1.333)
systems.description: Typographic scale using a 1.333 ratio (perfect fourth musical interval). Produces noticeable size jumps that create strong visual hierarchy. Starting from 16px base: 16, 21.3, 28.4, 37.9, 50.5. Well-suited for content-rich sites with clear heading levels.
principles: Multiply each step by 1.333 for the next size
Larger jumps create more dramatic heading hierarchy
May need to skip steps for less dramatic sub-hierarchies
Works well with serif and sans-serif typefaces alike
Consider fluid typography to smooth between breakpoints
when_to_use: Content-heavy editorial or documentation sites
Interfaces with 3+ heading levels needing clear distinction
Marketing pages with strong typographic hierarchy
Design systems where type hierarchy is a primary tool
avoid_when: Compact data-dense interfaces where large headings waste space
Mobile-first designs where large headings overflow small screens
Minimal interfaces with flat hierarchy
signals: Heading levels are hard to distinguish visually
Content pages lack clear information hierarchy
Type sizes jump inconsistently between heading levels
key_concepts: Ratio: 1.333 (perfect fourth)
Pairing: strong hierarchy needs fewer weight variations
Line height: 1.6 body, 1.3 subheading, 1.05 display

systems.name: Augmented Fourth Type Scale (1.414)
systems.description: Typographic scale using a 1.414 ratio (augmented fourth / tritone, equal to the square root of 2). Produces dramatic size jumps ideal for expressive, editorial, or display-heavy interfaces. Starting from 16px base: 16, 22.6, 32, 45.25, 64.
principles: Multiply each step by 1.414 for the next size
Very dramatic jumps — use only 4-6 steps total
Best for interfaces with few heading levels but strong impact
May need intermediate steps for body-adjacent sizes
Particularly effective for display and hero typography
when_to_use: Editorial and magazine-style layouts
Marketing and landing pages with hero typography
Interfaces prioritizing visual drama over density
Display-heavy designs with large hero text
avoid_when: Data-dense enterprise applications
Small screens where large sizes overflow
Interfaces with many heading levels (h1-h6 all needed)
Compact UI with limited vertical space
signals: Design calls for dramatic, editorial feel
Hero sections need impactful display type
Current scale feels too subtle for the brand personality
Few heading levels but need clear visual weight
key_concepts: Ratio: 1.414 (augmented fourth / square root of 2)
Double progression: each second step doubles (16, 32, 64)
Pairing: works well with light weights at large sizes

systems.name: Fluid Typography with Viewport-Responsive Sizing
systems.description: Typography that scales smoothly between minimum and maximum sizes based on viewport width using CSS clamp(). Eliminates abrupt size jumps at breakpoints and ensures optimal readability across all screen sizes without media queries.
principles: Define minimum size, preferred fluid size, and maximum size
clamp() provides built-in min and max bounds
Fluid scaling should be subtle — large swings feel unstable
Base size on viewport width (vw) with rem fallback
Test at extreme viewport widths to catch edge cases
when_to_use: Responsive websites needing smooth type scaling
Hero text that should be large on desktop, readable on mobile
Design systems targeting wide range of screen sizes
Replacing breakpoint-based font-size media queries
avoid_when: Fixed-dimension interfaces (embedded widgets, emails)
Applications where text size should remain constant
Contexts where viewport units behave unexpectedly (iframes)
Accessibility requirements mandate user-controlled text sizing only
signals: Font sizes jump abruptly at breakpoints
Hero text is too large on mobile or too small on desktop
Multiple media queries exist solely for font-size changes
Text readability varies significantly across screen sizes
key_concepts: clamp(min, preferred, max): font-size: clamp(1rem, 0.5rem + 2vw, 2.5rem)
Viewport units: vw for width-based, vi for inline-axis scaling
Preferred formula: calc(base-rem + vw-factor)
Generator: utopia.fyi for calculating fluid type scales
Container query units: cqi for component-level fluid type
Accessibility: clamp() with rem ensures user zoom still works
Lock points: viewport widths where min and max values kick in

systems.name: Web Font Loading Strategies
systems.description: Techniques for loading custom web fonts while minimizing layout shift, flash of invisible text (FOIT), and flash of unstyled text (FOUT). Covers font-display CSS property, preloading, font subsetting, and fallback font metric matching.
principles: Text should be visible as quickly as possible
Minimize layout shift when web fonts load
Subset fonts to reduce file size
Preload critical fonts in the document head
Match fallback font metrics to minimize CLS
when_to_use: Any website using custom web fonts
Performance optimization for font-heavy pages
Reducing Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) scores
Implementing brand typography on the web
avoid_when: Using only system fonts (no loading needed)
Offline-first applications with bundled fonts
Server-rendered applications where fonts are already cached
signals: Text is invisible for 1-3 seconds on first load (FOIT)
Layout shifts visibly when web font replaces fallback
Core Web Vitals CLS score is high due to fonts
Font files are large (>100KB per weight/style)
key_concepts: font-display: swap — show fallback immediately, swap when loaded
font-display: optional — use font only if already cached
font-display: fallback — brief invisible period, then fallback
Preload: <link rel='preload' href='font.woff2' as='font' crossorigin>
Subsetting: include only needed character ranges (latin, latin-ext)
Size-adjust: CSS property to match fallback font metrics
WOFF2: preferred format for best compression
Font loading API: document.fonts.ready for programmatic control

systems.name: Variable Fonts and Axes of Variation
systems.description: OpenType variable fonts contain multiple styles in a single file, with continuous variation along defined axes (weight, width, slant, optical size, and custom axes). Reduces HTTP requests and file size while enabling fine-grained typographic control.
principles: One file replaces multiple weight/style files
Continuous variation enables precise tuning (not just 400, 700)
Optical size axis adjusts detail for different font sizes
Custom axes enable brand-specific typographic expression
Performance benefit: one file vs many weight files
when_to_use: Projects using multiple weights of the same typeface
Designs requiring intermediate weights (450, 550)
Animated typography with weight or width transitions
Performance optimization to reduce font file count
avoid_when: Only one weight of a typeface is needed
Variable font file is larger than needed static files combined
Browser support requirements exclude variable font support
Design uses only standard weights (400, 700)
signals: Multiple font files loaded for same family (light, regular, medium, bold)
Design requires intermediate weight values
Font weight animation or transition is desired
Large number of font HTTP requests in waterfall
key_concepts: Registered axes: wght (weight), wdth (width), slnt (slant), ital (italic), opsz (optical size)
Custom axes: GRAD (grade), XHGT (x-height), any 4-char uppercase tag
CSS: font-variation-settings: 'wght' 450, 'wdth' 85
CSS: font-weight: 450 (direct property for wght axis)
Optical size: font-optical-sizing: auto for automatic adjustment
Animation: transition font-variation-settings for smooth weight changes
File formats: .woff2 with variations table

systems.name: Vertical Rhythm and Baseline Grid Alignment
systems.description: System for aligning text and elements to a consistent vertical grid, typically based on the body text line-height. Creates harmonious spatial rhythm down the page where all text sits on evenly spaced horizontal lines, and spacing between elements follows the same rhythm.
principles: All line-heights should be multiples of the baseline unit
Spacing (margins, padding) should be multiples of the baseline unit
Baseline unit is typically the body text line-height (e.g., 24px for 16px/1.5)
Headings may need adjusted line-height to land on the grid
Strict vertical rhythm trades off against practical flexibility
when_to_use: Content-heavy editorial and documentation layouts
Multi-column layouts where text should align across columns
Design systems prioritizing typographic precision
Print-inspired digital layouts
avoid_when: Component-heavy interfaces where content varies unpredictably
Dynamic content where rigid rhythm causes awkward spacing
Rapid prototyping where vertical rhythm is premature optimization
Interfaces with minimal text content
signals: Text in adjacent columns does not align horizontally
Spacing between sections feels inconsistent
Line-heights are arbitrary values without relationship to spacing
Typographic layout feels loose or ungrounded
key_concepts: Baseline unit: body line-height (typically 24px = 16px * 1.5)
Line-height multiples: headings at 1x, 1.5x, or 2x baseline unit
Margin/padding: multiples of baseline unit (24, 48, 72, 96)
Heading adjustment: use padding to push heading onto grid
CSS: line-height as unitless ratio (1.5) not fixed value
Debug overlay: repeating-linear-gradient for baseline visualization
Pragmatic rhythm: align key elements, allow flexibility elsewhere

systems.name: Inclusive Design
systems.description: Design for human diversity. Recognize exclusion, learn from diversity, solve for one extend to many. Permanent, temporary, and situational disabilities.
avoid_when: never avoid this
when_to_use: accessibility
diverse user base
global products
public services
principles: recognize exclusion
learn from diversity
solve for one extend to many
persona spectrum

systems.name: Design Sprint
systems.description: Compressed design process. Map the problem on Monday, sketch solutions Tuesday, decide Wednesday, prototype Thursday, test with users Friday.
avoid_when: ongoing iteration
small tweaks
well understood patterns
when_to_use: new product validation
critical feature decisions
cross team alignment
principles: time boxed
cross functional
real prototype
real users
decide dont debate
